name: Description of Subgroup Generated by a Set
name_norm: description of subgroup generated by a set
statement: If G is a group and X is a nonempty subset of G, then the subgroup ⟨X⟩ generated by X consists of all finite products a_1^{n_1} a_2^{n_2} ... a_t^{n_t} (a_i ∈ X; n_i ∈ ℤ). In particular, for every a ∈ G, ⟨a⟩ = {a^n | n ∈ ℤ}.
proof_sketch: Let H be the set of all such finite products. Show H is a subgroup of G containing X and contained in every subgroup that contains X. Therefore H ≤ ⟨X⟩ ≤ H, yielding equality.

name: Description of Principal Ideal
statement: Let R be a ring and a ∈ R. The principal ideal (a) consists of all finite sums of elements of the forms na (n ∈ Z), ra, as, and r a s (r,s ∈ R).
proof_sketch: Show the set S of all such finite sums satisfies the ideal criteria, contains a, and is contained in every ideal containing a; thus S = (a).

name: Equicardinality of finite transcendence bases
statement: Let F be an extension field of K. If S is a finite transcendence base of F over K, then every transcendence base of F over K has the same number of elements as S.
proof_sketch: Proof continues beyond the excerpt provided.

name: Quotient Monoid/Group by a Congruence
statement: Let R be an equivalence relation on a monoid G such that a1 ~ a2 and b1 ~ b2 imply a1 b1 ~ a2 b2 for all a_i, b_i ∈ G. Then the set G/R of equivalence classes with operation (a)(b) = ab is a monoid. Moreover, if G is a group, G/R is a group; if G is abelian, G/R is abelian.
proof_sketch: First, well-definedness: if a1 = a2 and b1 = b2, then a1 ~ a2 and b1 ~ b2, so a1 b1 ~ a2 b2, giving a1 b1 = a2 b2. Associativity: a (b c) = a(bc) = a(bc) = (ab)c = (ab)c = (a b) c. Identity: e = e_G gives a e = ae = a and e a = ea = a. If G is a group, then a a^{-1} = a a^{-1} = e, so a^{-1} is the inverse. Abelian property is inherited directly.

name: Theorem 1.6 (Structure of Subgroups of Finitely Generated Free Abelian Groups)
name_norm: structure of subgroups of finitely generated free abelian groups
statement: If F is a free abelian group of finite rank n and G is a nonzero subgroup of F, then there exists a basis {x_1, ..., x_n} of F, an integer r (1 ≤ r ≤ n), and positive integers d_1, ..., d_r such that d_1 | d_2 | ... | d_r and G is free abelian with basis {d_1 x_1, ..., d_r x_r}.
proof_sketch: By induction on n. If n=1, G = ⟨d_1 x_1⟩. For n>1, let S be the set of integers s such that some basis {y_i} yields an element in G with coefficient s for y_1. Let d_1 be the least positive element in S. Using Lemma 1.5, choose basis {x_1, y_2, ..., y_n} with d_1 x_1 ∈ G, set H = ⟨y_2, ..., y_n⟩, so F = ⟨x_1⟩ ⊕ H. Prove G = ⟨d_1 x_1⟩ ⊕ (G ∩ H). Apply the induction hypothesis to G ∩ H in H, obtaining a basis {x_2, ..., x_n} of H and d_2 | ... | d_r with G ∩ H = ⟨d_2 x_2, ..., d_r x_r⟩. Minimality of d_1 forces d_1 | d_2. Then {x_1, ..., x_n} and {d_1 x_1, ..., d_r x_r} are the required bases.

name: Characterization of Injective Modules over Rings with Identity (Baer's Criterion)
name_norm: characterization of injective modules over rings with identity (baers criterion
statement: Let R be a ring with identity. A unitary R-module J is injective if and only if for every left ideal L of R, any R-module homomorphism f: L -> J may be extended to an R-module homomorphism R -> J.
proof_sketch: If J is injective, the extension property follows directly. Conversely, assume extension property for left ideals. Given a monomorphism g: A -> B and f: A -> J, let S be the set of partial extensions h: C -> J with Im g ⊆ C ⊆ B. Partially order S by extension. S is nonempty since f∘g^{-1}: Im g -> J works. By Zorn's Lemma, a maximal element h: H -> J exists. If H ≠ B, pick b∈B\H, set L = { r∈R | rb∈H } a left ideal. Map r ↦ h(rb) extends to k: R->J by hypothesis. Let c = k(1_R). Define extension on H+Rb by a+rb ↦ h(a)+rc, contradicting maximality. Hence H=B.

name: Correspondence Between Contravariant and Covariant Functors
name_norm: correspondence between contravariant and covariant functors
statement: Every contravariant functor S: C → D induces a unique covariant functor S̄: C^{op} → D defined by S̄(A)=S(A) and S̄(f^{op})=S(f). Conversely, every covariant functor on C^{op} arises uniquely from a contravariant functor on C.
proof_sketch: Verification of functor axioms using the definition of opposite category composition.

name: Maschke's Theorem
statement: Let G be a finite group and k a field. The group algebra k[G] is semisimple if and only if the characteristic of k does not divide the order of G.
proof_sketch: If char(k) ∤ |G|, prove every k[G]-module is completely reducible by averaging a linear projection over the group action using the Reynolds operator. The converse is shown by considering the sum of all group elements.

name: Noether Normalization Lemma
statement: Let A be a finitely generated algebra over a field k. Then there exist algebraically independent elements y₁,…,y_d ∈ A such that A is a finitely generated module over the polynomial subring k[y₁,…,y_d].
proof_sketch: Induct on the number of generators. If the generators are algebraically dependent, replace them to lower the total degree of the relation, eventually obtaining algebraically independent elements over which A is finite.

name: Generalized Associative Law
statement: If an element of a group is constructed from a sequence x₁, x₂, …, xₙ in order by repeatedly inserting brackets and applying the operation, the element must equal (…((x₁ ∘ x₂) ∘ x₃)…) ∘ xₙ, independently of the bracketing.
proof_sketch: Induction on n. Write u = v ∘ w with v from x₁…xᵢ, w from xᵢ₊₁…xₙ. If w = xₙ, induction applies directly. Else w = w' ∘ xₙ, then u = (v ∘ w') ∘ xₙ and induction gives the result.

name: Sylow's Theorems
statement: Let G be a finite group of order p^a * m where p is prime and p does not divide m. Then (i) Every p-subgroup of G is contained in a subgroup of order p^a; in particular, Sylow p-subgroups always exist. (ii) If n_p is the number of Sylow p-subgroups, n_p ≡ 1 mod p. (iii) All Sylow p-subgroups are conjugate in G.
proof_sketch: Let X be the set of all subsets of G of size p^a. G acts on X by right multiplication. Show p does not divide |X| = C(p^a m, p^a) by examining powers of p in numerator and denominator. Hence there exists a G-orbit whose size is not divisible by p. For an element in this orbit, its stabilizer P has index not divisible by p, and its order divides p^a and is at least p^a, so |P|=p^a; thus a Sylow p-subgroup exists. For (ii), let P be a Sylow p-subgroup and let 𝒮 be the set of its conjugates. P acts on 𝒮 by conjugation; orbits have p-power size. If {P1} is an orbit of size 1, then P ≤ N_G(P1), PP1 is a p-group containing P, so P=P1. Thus {P} is the unique orbit of size 1, giving n_p ≡ 1 mod p. For (iii), if P2 is a p-subgroup not contained in any conjugate of P, then P2 acts on 𝒮; any P2-orbit would have size >1 (otherwise P2 ≤ P3), so all orbit sizes are divisible by p, making n_p ≡ 0 mod p, contradicting (ii). Hence every p-subgroup lies in a conjugate of P; in particular all Sylow p-subgroups are conjugate.

name: Nilpotent Extension Property
statement: Let P be a property of groups that is closed under forming extensions. If G is a nilpotent group and its abelianization G_ab has P, then G itself has P.
proof_sketch: Set F_i = γ_i G / γ_{i+1} G. Assume F_i has P. Then F_{i+1}, as a homomorphic image of F_i ⊗ G_ab, also has P. By induction every lower central factor has P. Since G is nilpotent, some γ_{c+1} G = 1, so G is built by repeated extensions from these factors. As P is closed under extensions, G has P.

name: Structure of the Ω-subgroup generated by a set
name_norm: structure of the ω-subgroup generated by a set
statement: If G is an Ω-group and X a nonempty subset, the Ω-subgroup Xᴼ generated by X consists of all elements of the form (x_{i₁}^{ε₁})ʷ¹ … (x_{i_r}^{ε_r})ʷʳ, where x_i ∈ X, ε_i = ±1, r ≥ 0, and each ω_i is a finite sequence of operators from Ω applied successively.
proof_sketch: By the method of 1.3.3: the intersection of all Ω-subgroups containing X is the smallest such subgroup; these elements are contained in every Ω-subgroup containing X and themselves form an Ω-subgroup, hence they constitute Xᴼ.

name: Existence and Uniqueness of Maximal Normal Centerless CR-subgroup
name_norm: existence and uniqueness of maximal normal centerless cr-subgroup
statement: In any group G there exists a unique maximal normal centerless CR-subgroup. Moreover, this subgroup is characteristic in G.
proof_sketch: Consider a chain of normal centerless CR-subgroups M_α. A simple direct factor S of some M_α is subnormal in the union J, so S ⊲ J. Since J is generated by normal nonabelian simple subgroups, J is a CR-group by 3.3.10. Zorn's Lemma gives a maximal such subgroup M. For any other N, let I = M ∩ N. By 3.3.12, M = M_1 × I with M_1 ⊲ G. Then MN = M_1N = M_1 × N is a centerless CR-group. By maximality N ≤ M, proving uniqueness. Characteristicity follows from uniqueness under automorphisms.
